articles.label: Public Records
articles.headline: Escambia County quiet on $2.1M settlement with former jail nurse
articles.dek: The county paid out seven figures in January. Six months later, commissioners still won't say why—and the personnel file has vanished from public records requests.
brief: Escambia County paid $2.1 million to settle claims brought by former jail nurse Denise Harmon in January 2026
The county has declined to release Harmon's personnel file, citing an ongoing 'administrative matter'—despite the settlement being finalized
Florida public records law requires disclosure of settlement agreements involving public funds, but the county has produced only a single redacted page
articles.body:
The check cleared on January 14. Two million, one hundred thousand dollars. Payable to Denise Harmon, a registered nurse who worked at the Escambia County Jail for eleven years before her termination in March 2024.

That much is visible in county financial records, buried in a quarterly disbursement report posted to the comptroller's website in February. The line item reads: "Legal Settlement—Personnel Matter—Harmon, D."

Everything else about why Escambia County taxpayers paid seven figures to a former corrections employee remains, six months later, officially unknowable.

The paper trail stops cold

The Flightline filed its first public records request on February 3, two days after the disbursement report appeared online. The request sought Harmon's personnel file, any complaints filed by or against her, and the settlement agreement itself.

Sixteen days later, County Attorney Alison Baxter responded with a single page: the signature block of the settlement agreement, with the settlement amount visible. Everything else—the factual recitals, the claims being released, the terms—had been redacted under black bars.

"The remainder of the document contains information exempt from disclosure under Florida law," Baxter wrote, citing statute 119.071, which covers personnel information and ongoing investigations.

Asked which specific exemption applied, Baxter declined to elaborate. Asked whether any investigation remained ongoing—given that the settlement had been paid—she did not respond.

The personnel file request fared worse. On March 9, after the statutory deadline had passed, the county responded that no responsive records existed.

Denise Harmon worked at the Escambia County Jail from 2013 to 2024. Eleven years. And according to Escambia County, no personnel file exists.

What former colleagues say

Harmon declined to comment for this story, citing the terms of her settlement. But three former jail employees, speaking on condition of anonymity because they still work for the county, described a pattern of conflict between Harmon and jail administrators that intensified in late 2023.

"She was documenting everything," one former colleague said. "Every time medical got overruled by custody, she wrote it up. They hated her for it."

Another described Harmon as "the nurse who actually read the policies" and said her termination came two weeks after she filed an internal complaint about medication distribution protocols.

The Flightline requested records of any internal complaints filed by Harmon. The county responded that such records, if they existed, would be exempt from disclosure as part of an ongoing administrative matter.

When asked what administrative matter could be ongoing after a settlement had been paid, the county did not respond.

The legal framework

Florida's public records law is among the broadest in the nation. Article I, Section 24 of the state constitution establishes a presumptive right of access to government documents, with narrow exemptions that must be specifically authorized by the legislature.

Settlement agreements involving public funds are generally public records. The Florida Supreme Court affirmed this in Tribune Co. v. Cannella (1990), holding that the public interest in knowing how tax dollars are spent outweighs privacy concerns in most personnel matters.

But the law contains carve-outs. Personnel evaluations, medical records, and documents related to ongoing investigations can be withheld. The question is whether those exemptions apply here—and whether the county is interpreting them correctly.

"The exemptions are supposed to be narrow," said Patricia Marsh, a media law attorney in Tallahassee who reviewed the county's responses at The Flightline's request. "Saying a personnel file doesn't exist for an eleven-year employee strains credulity. Either they destroyed it, which raises its own questions, or they're playing games with the request."

Marsh noted that under Florida law, personnel files must be retained for at least five years after separation. Harmon's termination occurred in March 2024—two years ago.

"That file should exist," Marsh said. "If it doesn't, someone needs to explain what happened to it."

Commissioners won't talk

The Flightline contacted all five Escambia County commissioners seeking comment on the settlement. Three did not respond. One, District 4 Commissioner Robert Bender, replied through a spokesperson that he could not discuss personnel matters. District 2 Commissioner Lumon May agreed to an interview but canceled twice before stopping communication.

The settlement was not voted on in any public meeting. Under Florida law, settlement agreements can be approved in closed session when litigation is pending or imminent, with the terms later ratified in public. A review of county commission minutes from October 2024 through February 2026 shows no public vote ratifying the Harmon settlement.

County Attorney Baxter, asked whether the settlement was properly approved, responded that "all legal requirements were followed" but declined to specify which meeting or mechanism was used.

The broader pattern

The Harmon settlement is the largest personnel-related payout in Escambia County in at least five years, based on available financial records. But it is not the only one.

Since 2021, the county has paid out at least $4.3 million in settlements related to the jail, including wrongful death claims, excessive force allegations, and medical negligence suits. Most of those settlements were disclosed through litigation records in federal court, where transparency requirements are stricter.

The Harmon case appears to have settled before any lawsuit was filed—or if a suit was filed, it was done under seal. Federal court records for the Northern District of Florida show no civil case naming Harmon as a plaintiff. State court records in Escambia County show the same.

This means the only public evidence that $2.1 million changed hands is a single line in a quarterly financial report.

What happens next

The Flightline has filed a formal appeal of the county's public records responses with the State Attorney's office, which has authority to investigate potential violations of Florida's public records law. A response is expected within 30 days.

The publication has also filed a separate request with the Florida Department of Law Enforcement, seeking any records related to investigations at the Escambia County Jail involving medical staff between 2023 and 2024.

Meanwhile, the county's silence continues. At the April 8 commission meeting, three residents asked about the settlement during public comment. Commission Chair Jeff Bergosh thanked them for their input and moved to the next agenda item.

No commissioner responded to the questions. The meeting adjourned at 6:47 p.m.

This story will be updated as additional records become available. Anyone with information about the Harmon settlement or conditions at the Escambia County Jail can contact The Flightline at tips@theflightline.com.

articles.label: Downtown
articles.headline: The hole on Palafox turns three
articles.dek: A demolished building, a stalled project, and a downtown block that's been dirt and chain-link since 2023. What happened to the promised mixed-use development at Palafox and Garden?
brief: The former site of the Brent Building at Palafox and Garden has sat vacant since demolition in April 2023
Developer Studer Properties cited construction cost increases and financing challenges for the delay
City officials say they have no timeline for when the promised mixed-use project will break ground
articles.body:
The chain-link fence has been there so long that morning glories have started climbing it. Behind the fence, at the corner of Palafox and Garden streets, sits what locals have come to call "the hole" — a dirt lot where the Brent Building stood until April 2023.

Three years ago this month, demolition crews tore down the four-story structure. Renderings showed what would replace it: a seven-story mixed-use development with ground-floor retail, 48 apartments, and a rooftop bar overlooking Pensacola Bay. Studer Properties promised groundbreaking by early 2024.

That groundbreaking never came.

"We drive by it every day," said Marcus Webb, who owns a photography studio two blocks north on Palafox. "At first you think, okay, construction takes time. But three years of nothing? That's not construction. That's abandonment."

What the developer says

Studer Properties, the development arm of Quint Studer's business portfolio, acknowledged the delays in a statement provided to The Flightline.

"Like many development projects nationwide, the Palafox and Garden project has faced significant headwinds," the statement read. "Construction costs increased approximately 34% between our initial projections and current estimates. We remain committed to this site and to downtown Pensacola."

The statement did not include a timeline for construction. A spokesperson declined a follow-up interview.

Property records show Studer Properties still owns the parcel, assessed at $1.2 million. The company has paid property taxes on the vacant lot each year — roughly $18,000 annually for dirt and chain-link.

City Hall's position

Pensacola City Councilwoman Teniadé Broughton, whose District 6 includes downtown, said she has raised the issue with city staff multiple times.

"There's only so much we can do," Broughton said in an interview. "It's private property. They're current on taxes. They're not violating any code. We can't force someone to build."

She paused.

"But I understand the frustration. This is our main street. This is the block visitors see. And it looks like we gave up on it."

City records show no active permits for the site. The last permit activity was a demolition permit issued in February 2023 and closed out that April.

Mayor D.C. Reeves, asked about the project at a recent press availability, said he had spoken with Studer Properties leadership "within the last few months" but declined to characterize those conversations.

"Private development moves on private timelines," Reeves said. "I'd love to see something there. Everyone would. But I'm not going to pretend the city has leverage we don't have."

The economics of waiting

Commercial real estate analysts say the Palafox site reflects a broader pattern in mid-size Southern downtowns.

"You had this wave of optimism in 2021, 2022," said Rachel Thibodeaux, a commercial broker with Levin Rinke who is not involved with the Studer project. "Money was cheap. Everyone thought post-COVID downtowns were going to boom. Then rates went up. Construction costs went up. And a lot of projects just stopped."

Thibodeaux said she knows of at least three other downtown Pensacola projects currently "in a holding pattern" — approved, permitted, but not moving.

"The math doesn't work right now for a lot of these deals," she said. "So you wait. You hope rates come down. You hope costs stabilize. And in the meantime, you've got a hole in the ground."

What was there before

The Brent Building, constructed in 1972, housed a rotating cast of tenants over its five decades — law offices, a bail bondsman, a short-lived coworking space. By 2020, the building was mostly vacant. Studer Properties acquired it in 2021 for $2.8 million, according to county records.

Some longtime downtown residents remember the site's earlier history. Before the Brent Building, a two-story commercial block from the 1920s occupied the corner. Before that, according to Pensacola Historical Society records, the site held a livery stable.

Now it holds weeds.

Waiting for something

On a recent Wednesday afternoon, a group of tourists paused at the corner of Palafox and Garden. One of them, a woman in a sun hat, peered through the chain-link.

"Is this where they're building something?" she asked her companion.

"I think so," he said. "Eventually."

They moved on, heading south toward the bay, past the restaurants and boutiques that fill the blocks where buildings still stand.

The fence stayed where it was. The morning glories kept climbing.

articles.label: Military
articles.headline: Blue Angels homecoming week brings 300,000 visitors—and a reckoning for Beach traffic
articles.dek: Pensacola Beach residents brace for the annual crush as the city and county still haven't agreed on a shuttle plan first proposed in 2019.
brief: The Blue Angels homecoming air show draws an estimated 300,000 visitors to Pensacola Beach over two days, with traffic backing up past the Bob Sikes Bridge for hours.
A regional shuttle system proposed in 2019 remains stalled over funding disputes between Escambia County and the Santa Rosa Island Authority.
Beach residents report being unable to leave their homes during peak show hours, with some calling the annual gridlock 'untenable.'
articles.body:
The line of brake lights stretched from Casino Beach past the toll plaza, past the Bob Sikes Bridge, past the Pensacola Beach Boulevard intersection and into Gulf Breeze proper. It was 9:47 a.m. on a Saturday in July, still two hours before the first F/A-18 Super Hornet would scream over Santa Rosa Sound. Parents fanned themselves in idling minivans. A man in a lifted truck gave up and executed a three-point turn across the median.

This is homecoming week on Pensacola Beach.

The Blue Angels' annual return to their home base at NAS Pensacola draws an estimated 300,000 spectators over the Friday practice show and Saturday performance—numbers that dwarf the island's infrastructure. The two-lane Pensacola Beach Road becomes a parking lot. The Bob Sikes Bridge, the only vehicle access point, transforms into a chokepoint that can trap visitors for three hours or more.

And seven years after Escambia County first floated a regional shuttle system to ease the crush, local governments still haven't agreed on who should pay for it.

'We become prisoners'

Janet Kellerman has lived on Ariola Drive for 22 years. She knows to stock her refrigerator the Wednesday before homecoming. She knows not to schedule anything that requires leaving the island between Thursday afternoon and Saturday night.

"We become prisoners in our own homes," Kellerman said. "I'm not complaining about the Blue Angels—I love watching them. But the traffic situation has gotten worse every single year, and nobody seems willing to actually fix it."

Kellerman said she tried to drive to a doctor's appointment in Gulf Breeze during last year's practice show. She turned back after sitting motionless on Via de Luna for 45 minutes.

The Santa Rosa Island Authority, which governs the Beach, has documented the problem extensively. A 2023 traffic study commissioned by SRIA found that vehicle throughput on the Bob Sikes Bridge drops to roughly 800 cars per hour during peak homecoming periods—less than half its normal capacity. The study attributed the slowdown to rubbernecking, illegal parking along the causeway and the sheer volume of vehicles attempting to access limited beach parking.

That same study recommended a park-and-ride shuttle system operating from multiple mainland locations, with dedicated bus lanes on the bridge approach. Estimated cost: $340,000 annually for the two-day event.

Seven years of proposals

The shuttle idea isn't new. Escambia County Commissioner Jeff Bergosh first raised it at a 2019 board meeting, suggesting the county partner with Escambia County Area Transit to run buses from the Pensacola Bay Center parking garage.

"We could take 5,000 cars off that bridge," Bergosh said at the time. "The technology exists. Other communities do this for major events. We're just choosing not to."

The proposal stalled over funding. SRIA argued that since the air show occurs over NAS Pensacola—federal property—the county should bear the transportation costs. The county countered that SRIA collects toll revenue from the Bob Sikes Bridge and should reinvest it in traffic mitigation. ECAT said it would need a funding guarantee before committing buses and drivers to a weekend operation.

In 2022, then-County Administrator Janice Gilley proposed a pilot program splitting costs three ways between the county, SRIA and the Pensacola Beach Chamber of Commerce. The chamber declined, citing concerns about liability and logistics.

A revised proposal in 2024 died when SRIA board member Brigette Brooks questioned whether shuttles would actually reduce traffic or simply shift the bottleneck to mainland parking areas.

"We've been having the same conversation for years," said Karen Sindel, who chairs the SRIA board. "Everyone agrees there's a problem. Nobody agrees on the solution or who should pay for it."

The Navy's limited role

NAS Pensacola operates the air show but has little control over civilian traffic patterns. Base spokesperson Lt. Cmdr. Rachel Torres said the Navy coordinates with local law enforcement on traffic control but cannot mandate how people access the Beach.

"We encourage spectators to arrive early and consider watching from locations other than Pensacola Beach," Torres said in an email. "The show is visible from many vantage points around the bay."

Those alternative locations—including NAS Pensacola itself, which opens its gates to the public during the show—have their own capacity limits. The base caps attendance at 40,000 for safety reasons. Fort Pickens, accessible only from the Beach, offers no traffic relief.

Gulf Breeze, which sits between the mainland and the island, has resisted suggestions that it host shuttle staging areas. City Manager Samantha Abell said Gulf Breeze lacks suitable parking facilities and shouldn't bear infrastructure costs for an event it doesn't host or benefit from.

"Our residents already deal with significant pass-through traffic during homecoming," Abell said. "Adding staging areas would only make that worse."

What other cities do

The shuttle impasse frustrates observers who point to successful models elsewhere.

The Chicago Air and Water Show, which draws two million spectators to Lake Michigan's shoreline, operates a comprehensive transit plan involving dedicated bus routes, extended train service and temporary traffic restrictions. San Francisco Fleet Week coordinates with BART and Muni to discourage driving. Even Daytona Beach—not known for sophisticated urban planning—runs shuttle buses during major events at the International Speedway.

"Pensacola acts like this is an unsolvable problem," said Marcus Webb, a transportation consultant who reviewed the 2023 SRIA study. "It's not. It's a coordination problem and a funding problem, and those are both solvable if the political will exists."

Webb estimated that a properly designed shuttle system could remove 8,000 to 12,000 vehicles from the Bob Sikes Bridge corridor over the two-day event—enough to restore near-normal traffic flow.

This year's plan

Escambia County Sheriff's Office will deploy its standard homecoming traffic plan this July: deputies stationed at key intersections, no-parking enforcement along the causeway, and variable message signs urging early arrival.

It's the same plan the department has used for a decade.

"We do the best we can with the resources we have," said ECSO spokesperson Amber Southard. "But we can't create more road capacity."

SRIA has added 200 temporary parking spaces near the Gulfside Pavilion, bringing total Beach parking to roughly 4,200 spots. At peak attendance, demand exceeds 15,000.

The agency will also test a new real-time parking availability system this year, with electronic signs at the toll plaza showing how many spaces remain. The idea is to discourage drivers from crossing the bridge when parking is full.

Whether anyone will turn around remains to be seen.

Kellerman, the Ariola Drive resident, isn't optimistic. She's already scheduled her July grocery run for the Tuesday before the show.

"I've given up expecting them to fix it," she said. "I just plan around it now. That's life on the Beach during homecoming."
